md: The **derivative** is the central object of differential calculus. It captures one of the most useful ideas in mathematics: **the instantaneous rate of change** of a function. Geometrically, it's the slope of the tangent line.
## 1. From Average to Instantaneous Rate of Change
The **average rate of change** of $f$ over $[a, a + h]$ is the slope of the secant line:
As $h \to 0$, this secant slope approaches the slope of the **tangent line** at $x = a$ ‚Äî the instantaneous rate of change. That limit IS the derivative.
This is the **limit definition of the derivative**, sometimes called the 'difference quotient' definition. Memorize it ‚Äî every derivative rule traces back to this.
## 2. Try It ‚Äî See the Tangent Line
Slide the point in the widget below. The red tangent line's slope IS the derivative at that x-value. Switch functions and feel the relationship.
## 3. Computing a Derivative from the Definition
**Sanity check:** at $x = 1$, slope = 2. The widget above shows exactly this.
Several equivalent notations for the derivative of $f$:
**$f'(x)$** ‚Äî Lagrange's notation (most common in intro calc).
**$\dfrac{df}{dx}$** or **$\dfrac{dy}{dx}$** ‚Äî Leibniz's notation. Useful for emphasizing what variable you're differentiating with respect to.
**$Df$** ‚Äî operator notation (less common in intro classes).
All mean the same thing. Use whichever is clearest in context.
The tangent line to $f$ at $x = a$ has:
**Example.** Find the tangent to $f(x) = x^2$ at $x = 3$.

blocks.title: What the derivative tells you
blocks.body: **Geometrically:** slope of the tangent line. **Physically:** instantaneous rate of change (e.g., velocity is the derivative of position; acceleration is the derivative of velocity). **Statistically:** sensitivity (how much $f$ changes per unit change in $x$). The same number means all of these ‚Äî depends on what you're modeling.

md: A ball's height (in feet) at time $t$ (seconds) is $s(t) = -16t^2 + 64t$.
Using the limit definition (or the rules we'll prove next): $s'(t) = -32t + 64$.
**Velocity at $t = 2$:** $v(2) = 0$ ft/sec ‚Äî the ball is momentarily stopped at the peak of its arc.
